prompt: A CEO is told: raise the ad budget by $10,000 and sales will rise by about $40,000. Which use of regression is that?
why: Section 1.1 lists three uses in rising order of what they ask of the data: description reads a slope off data you already have, prediction guesses an unseen outcome, and causal reasoning says what an intervention would do. Claiming that changing the budget will move sales is a causal claim, the hardest of the three, because a fit to observed data measures association, not cause.

prompt: Galton's fitted line is child = 22.64 + 0.64 * midparent. The slope 0.64 is below 1. What does that tell you?
options: Tall parents have children who are tall but closer to average than the parents.
Children exactly match their parents' height.
Taller parents tend to have shorter children.
Child height is unrelated to parent height.
why: Section 1.2: a slope of 1 would mean children match their parents' deviations from average exactly, and a slope of 0 would mean no link at all. The value 0.64 sits between them, so each extra inch of mid-parent height goes with only about two-thirds of an inch of extra child height. The children are pulled toward the middle. That is regression to the mean written as one number.

prompt: Match each part of the model Y = f(X) + epsilon to what it means.

pairs.meaning: the noise the predictor cannot explain
the fixed unknown truth in the population
the best guess computed from a sample

why: Section 1.3: a statistical model splits every response into a systematic signal f(X) and a random noise epsilon. The signal is what we estimate; the noise is honest variation the predictors do not capture. Inside the signal, a parameter like beta_1 is the true value we never see directly, while an estimate like b_1 is the number we compute from data as its best guess.

prompt: One sentence about the model Y = f(X) + epsilon is wrong. Tap it.

sentences.text: The systematic part f(X) is the average response the predictor explains.
The random part epsilon is real variation the predictors do not capture.
The error term means the model made a mistake that a better model would remove.
A parameter is the unknown truth; an estimate is what we compute from a sample.

why: Section 1.3: the random part epsilon is not a failure of the model. It is the honest admission that lot size does not fix hours exactly and temperature does not fix O-ring damage exactly. A good model estimates the signal and describes the size of the noise; it does not delete the noise, because the noise is real.

prompt: These are the 11 points of Anscombe's set 1, a genuine straight-line relationship. Drag the sliders until the SSE meter turns green.
why: Section 1.4 (Example 1.4): all four Anscombe sets share the same fitted line, y-hat = 3.0 + 0.5x. Set 1 is the one where that line is honest, so a slope near 0.5 with an intercept near 3 drives the sum of squared vertical gaps toward its minimum. Least squares is exactly this search for the line with the smallest total squared error.

prompt: This is Anscombe's set 3: ten points on a tight line plus one that does not fit. Tap the outlier.
why: Section 1.4 (Example 1.4): set 3 is a straight line derailed by a single outlier. Ten of the eleven points sit almost exactly on a line, but the point at x = 13 sits far above it and drags the fitted slope away from the other ten. The summary numbers are blind to this; a two-second plot is not, which is why plotting is a safety check you run every single time.

prompt: Put the modeling workflow the whole course repeats into order.
steps: ASK a question and get the data
EXPLORE it with plots and summaries
CHECK whether it can be trusted
USE it to interpret, predict, or decide
why: Section 1.7: the course spine is ASK, EXPLORE, FIT, CHECK, USE. You explore before fitting so the model matches what you see, and you check the model before you use it, because a model that fails its check should be sent back and rebuilt rather than pushed forward. Every chapter is one trip around this loop.

prompt: Across the summer, ice-cream sales and drownings rise and fall together. One sentence below is wrong. Tap it.

sentences.text: The correlation between ice-cream sales and drownings is strong and real.
Banning ice cream would reduce the number of drownings.
Hot weather is a confounder that drives both variables up together.
A strong association here need not mean one variable causes the other.

why: Section 1.4: hot weather is a lurking variable that pushes up ice-cream sales and, separately, sends more people into the water where a few drown. The two are strongly associated with no causal link between them, so banning ice cream would change nothing. Reading an association as cause is the most common and most expensive error in applied statistics.

prompt: Now that anyone can fit a model in one line of code, which skill does the course call the scarce one?
options: Fitting a model as fast as possible
Judging whether a model can be trusted
why: Section 1.6: fitting used to be the hard part, but a machine will now fit a regression, a random forest, or a neural network on request. The scarce skill is judging the result: is the model appropriate, do its assumptions hold, is a confounder lurking, is the confident prediction actually an extrapolation off the edge of the data? Deciding whether to trust an answer is the work this book trains.

prompt: Every shuttle flight on record launched at 53F or warmer, but the Challenger decision was for about 31F. Why is predicting O-ring damage at 31F dangerous?
options: The dataset has too few flights to fit any line.
31F is an extrapolation far below every temperature ever flown.
O-ring damage cannot be measured in cold weather.
Temperature has nothing to do with O-ring damage.
why: The opening vignette and Section 1.6 make this the book's warning about extrapolation. The temperature-damage pattern was only ever observed at 53F and warmer, so the shape of any curve below 53F is an assumption the data cannot check. A model still returns a confident-looking number at 31F, but that number extends the summary past the edge of all experience.
